fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

RIETHMULLER J:

The applicant wife and the respondent husband are seeking both parenting and property orders following the breakdown of their marriage.
The husband was born in 1974 and was 51 years of age at the time of trial. The wife was born in 1982 and was 43 years of age at the time of trial.
The wife seeks a change in the primary care of the parties’ youngest child, who has not seen the wife since 15 May 2025 (Progress Report of Mr FF dated 30 July 2025) and has refused to engage with her since separation. Whilst the wife sought a relationship with the eldest child she did not press for orders in that respect, given his age and reluctance to see her.
The wife seeks orders for the youngest child to live with her and a moratorium on the husband’s time with the child. The husband seeks sole parental responsibility, that the children live with him and that spend time arrangements for the child with the wife begin with supervised phone calls, and face-to-face time be considered only after further family therapy.
The wife alleges the husband was verbally and emotionally abusive throughout their marriage and has poisoned the children against her. The husband claims that the wife’s conduct has resulted in the children rejecting her.
The wife also sought, in her amended application, the totality of the sale proceeds from the former matrimonial home at F Street Town E, NSW (the “[Town E] property”). The husband sought 70 per cent of the proceeds of sale, with the wife to receive the remaining 30 per cent, a cash payment from the wife in the sum of $53,651 for a variety of debts and fees including school fees and ATO debt and 50 per cent of the wife’s superannuation entitlements from her Super Fund 1 account.
For the reasons that follow orders will be made for the child to live with the wife and for the wife to receive 60 per cent of the remaining assets (including superannuation).

BACKGROUND

In 1996 the husband started a business.
The parties met in 2000 and commenced living together in February 2001.
In 2003 they purchased land in Town G for $175,000 and built a house.
In late 2005 the parties married.
It appears clear that there was tension from early in the marriage around the extent of intimacy that the parties engaged in, which was insufficient from the wife’s perspective. Beyond noting that the marriage had not been a contented one for the parties, even from earliest days, there is little relevance in traversing the details of their intimate relationship.
In 2005 or 2006 the parties sold the Town G property for $465,000 and purchased land at Suburb H for $210,000 and built a house. They moved into the Suburb H property at the start of 2007.
In 2008 X was born. He is now 17 years of age.
In 2009 the parties subdivided the Suburb H property and sold part of the land to a neighbour for $190,000.
In around early 2010 the wife had a brief affair with a man from her workplace. She said that she was remorseful, disclosed the affair to the husband, and resigned from the employment. The husband slept in X’s room for a period. However the parties did not separate but persevered with their relationship.
In October 2011, the husband applied for an ADVO against the wife for an incident where the wife was alleged to have kicked the husband in the back as the husband was lying down while in the presence of X (see husband’s affidavit filed 16 July 2025, p.10 paragraph 41 and p.81). The wife denies that the incident occurred. The wife says that the following day the paternal grandmother told her that if she ever left the husband she should not think that she would “get custody of [X]” (wife’s affidavit filed 23 June 2025, p.30 paragraph 236).
In April 2012 there was an incident where the wife says that the husband threw a full container of washing liquid at her when swearing at her in front of X (wife’s affidavit filed 23 June 2025, p.30 paragraph 238). She says that she suffered significant bruising. The police became involved and the husband later obtained a letter from his GP, Dr B (Exhibit 5) where the GP said that the husband “has mentioned to me over the past few years that [the wife] has been coming out with bruising to different areas of her body. he has been concerned as to the cause of this. I have not seen this myself” (sic).
In September 2012 the parties sold the Suburb H property for $720,000 and purchased a property at Town J. The following year they sold the Town J property and purchased the former matrimonial home located at Town E for $762,000.
In 2015 Y was born. He is now 10 years of age.
In March 2016 the husband had an accident. He suffered significant injuries to his leg. Following surgery, the husband was in hospital for a month. Thereafter he was unable to walk for six months, and then needed crutches for some time after that. Fortunately he is now able to walk without crutches, but still suffers disabilities as a result of his injuries. He is unable to work and now receives income protection payments. The husband received a compensation payment of $1,038,000 from a medical negligence claim that arose from an operation the husband underwent for his injured leg. The husband closed his business as a result of his injuries.
In October 2016 the wife returned to full time employment.
From 2017 the husband says that he took greater responsibility for the day-to-day care of the children. The wife joined a gym and became more focused upon her health and fitness, engaging in running events. It seems that by this point the marriage had not continued to be a satisfactory one for the parties, as the husband recounts that X had told him of a love letter that he found in the wife's car, in the wife's handwriting, addressed to another man sometime in 2018.
During 2018 the wife went on holiday to India with a friend.
In January 2019 the husband says that X reported that he had seen the wife taking photos of herself naked, which the husband said left X distressed.
In 2021, X commenced high school. The husband recounts that he regularly took him to sports around 3 to 4 nights per week (with Y accompanying them) and the wife attended on Friday nights. The husband says that in 2021 or 2022, the wife began to have increasingly noticeable mood swings, although in the context of the case as a whole, it seems more likely that this reflects the breakdown of their relationship during this period. In late 2021, the husband was assessed by an occupational therapist in the home, as a result of his ongoing impairment due to the injuries from the accident some years earlier.
By 2022 the relationships within the family had broken down considerably, as is apparent from a number of incidents that were the focal points of evidence at trial:
(a) In 2022 (the year after X commenced high school) X alleges that the wife sprayed oil in his face in the presence of Y (the “Cooking Spray Incident”). The wife denies the allegations.

(b) On 6 April 2022 a further incident occurred where it is alleged that the wife hit X with a plastic serving spoon of mashed potato (the “Mashed Potato Incident”) after he swore at the wife when she was attempting to serve him mashed potato at the dinner table. The husband says that the wife was insisting that X have mashed potato and he was refusing, and that the force broke the spoon and left mild burns from the potato. The wife denies the incident occurred as alleged.

(c) The husband alleges that on 29 July 2022 the wife attempted to push him down the stairs and that X intervened. The husband says that Y was present.

(d) The paternal grandmother says that she saw the wife strike the husband in the arm with a closed fist when preparing dinner sometime in 2022.

The husband says that on 12 August 2022 the parental grandmother lent him $28,000 and that he repaid $25,000 the following month.
In early 2023 the husband says he considered moving out of the home but couldn’t find a suitable rental property.
The parties took a family holiday to City K in mid-January 2023 however it was marked by many disputes between the wife and X.
In February 2023 the parties separated, but lived separately under the one roof at the Town E property. By this time, X was aligned with the husband, ignoring the wife or telling her “fuck off. Go away. We don’t want you here” (wife’s affidavit filed 23 June 2025, p.11 paragraph 55). The wife says that X’s behaviour toward her was worse when the husband was present.
Both parties set out the interactions at the end of the relationship, although in different terms. The wife recounts telling the husband she was ending the relationship, and him pleading with her to stay, offering to make changes and even offering her an “open” relationship. The husband describes the wife saying she didn’t want to be stuck with him and referring to his leg injury, and alleges that the wife raised the idea of an open relationship. I prefer the wife’s version of these events. I accept that the husband pressed the wife to move out once it was clear that the relationship had ended and that he started taking on all of the child-caring duties, at times excluding the wife.
In early March 2023 the husband says that Y overheard the wife talking about another man and a couple of weeks later, when playing on the wife’s phone with X, saw sexual messages between the wife and another man in her Snapchat app. The husband says that on one occasion when X and Y were looking at the wife’s phone, they found conversations between her and others that included intimate messages and a photograph of the wife topless. The husband also says that the wife called the husband a “fat fucking cunt”.
From this point on it appears that the husband intervened when the wife wished to engage Y in activities, telling her that Y didn’t want to participate, or asking Y a leading question to elicit Y’s acquiescence to claims that he did not want to go with the wife.
In April 2023 the wife engaged a Family Dispute Resolution Practitioner, Mr GG. A s 60I certificate was issued in May 2023 as the husband refused to attend mediation.
The last time that either of the children spent time with the wife was on 9 May 2023. She says that on that date she told the husband that she had secured alternative accommodation and was moving out from the Town E property that weekend.
On 10 May 2023 the husband took the children to stay at the paternal grandmother’s home in Town L. Y was not sent to school on the Friday (which was the Friday of the Mother’s Day weekend when there was an event for mothers and children at the school). The wife had sent texts saying that the children should come home (wife’s affidavit filed 23 June 2025, p.16 at paragraph 86)
The husband took the children to the police station where X made a complaint about the wife, which led to ADVO proceedings and charges concerning the Mashed Potato Incident, the Cooking Spray Incident, and claims she attempted to push the husband down the stairs. The husband was quite firm that “ultimately” it was X who “decided to report the assaults to police” (husband’s affidavit filed 16 July 2025, p.19 paragraph 93).
On [...] May 2023 the wife was served with a provisional ADVO and charged with three counts in respect to alleged incidents that occurred in 2022.
On 25 May 2023 the wife commenced proceedings in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2), seeking an urgent interim hearing for parenting orders. The application for urgency was refused by a registrar and the matter was listed for a first directions hearing on 3 July 2023.
In the meantime, on 7 June 2023, the husband completed a Parenting After Separation course.
On 3 July 2023 interim orders were made for the wife to spend time with Y supervised by M Contact Centre, and have telephone time once a week on a Wednesday between 5.00 pm and 5.30 pm. Arrangements were put in place to obtain a short form child impact report from Dr C.
On 8 August 2023 the wife completed the Parenting After Separation course.
On 12 August 2023, at the first supervised visit that was scheduled after the interim orders were made, Y refused to get out of the car. Y refused to get out of the car on the second visit on 26 August 2023. On 1 September 2023 M Contact Centre suspended the supervised time arrangements.
The parties attended interviews with Dr C, who was jointly appointed to undertake a Child Impact Report, on 21 September 2023.
In the interim the husband had made arrangements for Y to attend upon Dr B, a psychologist. In October 2023 the wife telephoned Dr B however, the psychologist declined to see the wife. Soon after, on 23 November 2023 the wife’s solicitor received a letter from Dr B stating that in her opinion, it was not in Y and X’s best interests to see the wife (even though Dr B had not seen the wife or spoken to her).
On 10 November 2023 the wife attended one of X’s sports games. She says that she was standing at the rear of the field to watch (not having told the husband she was attending). When the husband and Y came close by she said hello to Y, or on the husband’s version called out to him. It appears that X spoke to the team manager who then approached the wife to ask her to leave, which she did after some discussion. The husband says that X told him that he did not want anyone at sports to know about the ADVO.
On 23 November 2023 the Child Impact Report by Dr C was released and the parties had an interim hearing before a registrar on 29 November 2023. On 8 December 2023 the registrar ordered the parties to attend family therapy with Ms D (who was to determine when and how supervised visits should recommence), and for the parties to attend mediation in relation to financial matters.
On 19 December 2023 Dr C informed the ICL that interviews were tentatively booked for 2 April 2024, however, the husband’s then-solicitor was unable obtain the husband’s instructions on the draft letter of instructions prepared by the ICL. The ICL sought that the matter be listed before a registrar, which resulted in consent for the appointment of Dr C to prepare a Full Expert Report being made on 22 December 2023. By this point there had been 10 court orders issued in the matter.
On 23 January 2024 an application was filed by the wife to review the registrar’s decision to suspend Y’s supervised time with the wife and for time to resume if recommended by Ms D. Judge Vasta made orders by consent for supervised visits to be reinstated between Y and the wife. Another three sets of orders were made by a registrar in February, March and May 2024, prior to the wife’s criminal proceedings being adjourned part-heard in the Local Court on [...] May 2024.
Unfortunately, the criminal charges against the wife were not listed for hearing until [...] March 2024. The hearing was not completed and had to be stood over part-heard to [...] May 2024.
Despite the apparently implacable refusal of Y to spend even supervised time with the wife, the husband offered time supervised by the child’s aunt.
In March 2024 the parties attended a property mediation. Given the parenting issues it was a pointless process, as the care of Y would inevitably be significant to any property settlement.
On 26 March 2024 the wife completed the Tuning into Teens course and the husband completed it on 28 March 2024.
The preparation of the proceedings was further delayed when the husband sought to have interviews with Dr C deferred until after the criminal proceedings and did not pay the invoice. The interviews scheduled for 23 April 2024 did not take place.
On 16 May 2024 the wife filed an application seeking orders for the husband to comply with the orders made on 22 December 2023 for payment of Dr C’s invoice and various financial orders. On 20 May 2024 Orders were made for the parties to attend interviews at a time and place as scheduled by Dr C. Interviews were then rescheduled for 9 July 2024.
On [...] May 2024 the Local Court magistrate was not available to continue hearing the criminal proceedings and the matter was stood over to [...] November 2024.
Soon after the adjournment of the criminal proceedings, on [...] July 2024, the husband’s solicitor informed Dr C that husband’s mother was in hospital and the children were unwell. This resulted in the interviews being rescheduled to 30 July 2024 (a delay of over three months from the original interview appointments). Fortunately, the parties attended their interviews on 30 July 2024 with Dr C.
On 22 July 2024 Orders were made by consent restraining the husband from removing Y from his primary school and restraining him from changing the children’s residence to a place more than 50 kilometres from Y’s school. The registrar also made orders on 19 July 2024 for the sale of the Town E property, a partial property settlement to the wife of $150,000 and for a payment to N Company of $88,172.28 to discharge their caveat on the Town E property (securing litigation funding for the wife).
On 28 August 2024 the wife completed the Circle of Security course.
The Town E property sold for $1,844,000 in September 2024 with a settlement date of [...] December 2024.
The wife received a partial property settlement in the sum of $238,000. The balance of the funds from the sale of the home were deposited into a Controlled Monies Account (CMA) pending final order (and are currently in a controlled monies account with the husband's present solicitors).
A further three sets of court orders were made by a registrar prior to the matter being listed for a Compliance and Readiness Hearing on 22 November 2024.
On 24 October 2024 Dr C’s report was released to the parties.
The family law proceedings were then listed on 22 November 2024 for a Compliance and Readiness Hearing. In the 19 months that the matter had been pending there were 20 court orders, however the wife had not yet had any supervised time with the child Y. At the Compliance and Readiness Hearing, the matter was transferred to Division 1. Notations were made that the husband refused to attend family therapy with Ms D.
On 5 December 2024 the wife filed a further Application in a Proceeding seeking orders for the re-engagement of Ms D for family therapy. Despite the matter having been transferred to Division 1 on 22 November 2024, a Division 2 registrar listed this application before another registrar for interim hearing on 22 January 2025. The listing in Division 2 was then vacated and the matter listed before me on 10 February 2025 for interim hearing and directions.
In late 2024 the wife was found guilty of three assault charges and the ADVO was ordered to remain in place until May 2025.
In late 2024 the wife appealed against the decision.
By late 2024, the husband sought the discharge of the single expert whose report was not favourable to his case.
On 10 February 2025 the matter was listed before me for an interlocutory hearing. I dismissed the husband’s application to discharge Dr C as the single expert, restrained the parties from having Y further attend upon Dr B (given that her involvement had not led to any changes in Y’s attitude towards the wife), and required the parties to provide Dr C’s report to both Dr B and Ms D to enable them to provide advice or reports to assist in the management of the proceedings, and for the parties to make enquiries as to the availability of Mr FF to undertake family therapy. On 17 February 2025 I made orders for the parties to attend upon Mr FF for family therapy commencing on [...] April 2025.
On 10 March 2025 final hearing dates were fixed for 4 August 2025, and orders made for the husband to facilitate and encourage the child, Y, to attend upon Mr FF for family therapy in accordance the Orders of 17 February 2025. The parties provided agreed Trial Directions which were made by consent (in chambers) on 10 March 2025.
On 18 March 2025 the husband completed a Positive Parenting Program and on 4 June 2025 completed the Circle of Security Parenting program.
Dr C enquired of the court as to the appropriate approach to be taken with respect to answers to questions that he had been sent by the husband’s solicitors in his capacity as the single expert witness. The matter came back before on 25 March 2025 and I ordered that the answers be provided to all of the parties, and that Dr C’s reports be provided to Mr FF together with a report that had then been prepared by Dr B.
Despite the extensive litigation and orders with respect to the wife’s time with Y being supervised, on [...] May 2025 the husband returned to the Local Court seeking a variation of the AVO, which was granted on an interim basis.
On [...] May 2025 at a Local Court mention the police informed the wife that they were not seeking to extend the AVO. However, the husband sought to file additional evidence, and orders were made for filing and serving evidence.
In mid-2025 the wife’s appeal against the convictions was heard in the District Court at Suburb O. The appeal was allowed on all grounds and the wife acquitted.
On 13 June 2025 the husband filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking that the hearing scheduled for August 2025 (some 27 months since the wife had filed the proceedings) be adjourned to permit more time for family therapy to occur. I heard this application on 30 June 2025 and declined to change the trial dates. I also made orders by consent for the purposes of ensuring that the husband arrange for Y to attend upon Mr FF for joint sessions with the wife on dates specified in the order (10, 17 and 24 July 2025) and at all other times as directed by Mr FF. An order was also made for Mr FF to provide a short progress report for the final hearing.
On 4 July 2025 I made orders in chambers releasing the District Court file for inspection by the parties. On 7 July 2025, again in chambers, I released Dr C’s addendum report to the parties.
On 8 July 2025 I made orders for the release of $50,000 to the husband by way of partial property settlement from the controlled monies account.
The first joint session between Y and the wife with Mr FF was scheduled for 10 July 2025: Y refused to participate in the joint session. The same thing occurred on 17 July 2025. The joint session scheduled for 24 July 2025 was cancelled by Mr FF. On 31 July 2025 Mr FF released a Progress Report.

WITNESSES

The wife

The wife gave some brief evidence in chief to confirm that she had moved to a new home closer to Y’s school, with the home having four bedrooms. She believes the home is an appropriate residence in an estate with bike paths. She explained that her current partner is not living with her but has gone back to his home for six months to see how the relationships develop.
The wife works in a managerial position, and her employer has assisted with her moving regions to place her in a better position for these proceedings. She currently leases the property she is in for 12 months.
The wife said that if Y was placed with her, she would take four weeks off work, and later in the trial confirmed that she would book accommodation for a holiday location where they had previously had positive experiences for the first period of time that Y was staying with her. She understood that Y is likely to be distressed if a change of care was ordered, and believes she is able to care for him and comfort him.
It seems that the closest the wife has been to Y (physically) was two weeks before the trial, where she was sitting on the couch in the family therapist’s office and Y came to the doorway.
The wife doesn't believe that Y is likely to run away once he is with her but is alive to this as an issue. She is also alive to the proposition that Y will likely be upset to be separated from X. Although, I note that the age difference is such that X is likely to be spending much less time with Y at this point in their lives.
The wife accepted that she no longer has any communication with X, but said that she hopes this will change as time passes.
The wife was cross-examined about her relationship with her new partner, Mr P, and said that she was expecting him to come and eventually live with her, although this was, in her view, many months away. She agreed that she hadn't spoken to Y about her partner Mr P, although this is hardly surprising given the nature of the relationship between her and Y at present. Indeed, had she raised it, it seems it may well have been an inappropriate time, given the circumstances.
I formed the view that she had a reasonable level of insight into the difficulties she is likely to confront if Y is placed in her care and that she has taken active steps to make preparations to facilitate any change in care arrangements as best as might reasonably be expected. I accept that her and her current partner have considered the circumstances and are mindful of the child’s needs if a change in care were to be ordered.
She explained the difficulties that the parties had confronted following the husband's injuries, pointing out that he had struggled to scale the stairs and said that he only cooked if she was unable to, for example, when she was sick or had her period. Clearly two of the disputed incidents (the Cooking Spray Incident and the Mashed Potato Incident) occurred when she was in the process of preparing meals.
She said that in 2022 the sports training was only two nights per week and that the game was on Friday night. She said she would attend although it would be late for Y. She accepted that the husband had taken the children for haircuts during this period.
The wife maintained that she had attended all of the parent-teacher nights and that she later had her access to the school portal removed as a result of steps taken by the husband.
In the period from 2017 to 2019, she explained that she was struggling with the relationship between her and the husband which had led her to become more involved in sport. She disputed that this may have led the husband to think that she was moving away from the family, explaining that she saw it more as him becoming increasingly depressed due to the disabilities following his injuries. She thought that the children were proud of her when she competed in sporting events and that they were happy to see the medallions that she won. However, this was not a happy time for her as there were arguments with the husband, sometimes in front of X. She said that, at times she would get upset and cry, and the husband would get angry.
She said that she has spoken to Y on the telephone but assumed that he was in the presence of others as there were often long pauses.
She denied having longstanding mental health issues, saying that she cannot think of anything bad or traumatic that occurred in childhood. However, close to the wedding, she had a falling‑out with her family and had had some anxiety at the time of the wedding.
She said that when she is upset, she gets emotional. Her view of the husband was that he could not accept things that she was telling him, and she believed he was acting inappropriately. She denied ever saying “I wonder if [X] loves me”. She was unaware that the husband had spoken to the GP about her potentially having post-natal depression. She said that she had always been threatened by the husband that, if they separated, their children would not live with her.
It seems that the marriage had not been a happy one for a long time. She had had an affair in 2009, which she had disclosed to the husband the next day; however, their marriage had continued for some time after that.
Her evidence concerning her relationship with her parents was vague to the point of evasiveness. When asked about whether her parents had brought court proceedings against her, the precise details never became clear, although it seems to have involved a claim concerning furniture and $12,000. Around 2009, it seems the police took out an apprehended violence order against her father.
She denied she had kicked the husband in the back when they were walking with X on one occasion and denied that she had put petrol in the fuel tank of the husband's diesel vehicle. It appears that there was an argument in October 2011 when she threw her ring over a fence and off a cliff. She also recalled a time when she became panicked when X had locked himself in the car when he was younger, but did not recall the husband coming to assist, nor feeling that her anxiety had been at a point where she needed to attend upon a doctor for assistance.
She was cross-examined about a friendship she had with a woman referred to as “[Ms Q]” and whether she had told Ms Q that she was terminating their friendship. She explained that she did not think that Ms Q was a positive person but said that she could not recall if she had told Ms Q that their friendship was over.
She accepted that she had written the letter that X had found in the car to a gentleman friend and that X may have seen it as a love letter; although she said the first she knew of this was reading the husband's affidavit, as X did not speak to her about it. Whilst she trusted the husband not to cheat upon her, she accepted that the husband may not have trusted her in that regard, given the events that had taken place.
She agreed that in 2018, and after she had been on a trip to India, she had told the husband she was staying with a friend in Sydney when she in fact went to visit someone in Melbourne.
She accepted that, in 2019, she had left her phone with X during a running event but said that the husband would encourage X to view her phone whenever he could. She said there were no photographs on the phone that were not on Instagram and that she had a friend, Mr R, at that time when she was running in events described as “[T Race]”. She did not recall X ever bringing any of these issues up with her.
It is apparent that when X was behaving badly towards her, for example swearing at her, the husband would never intervene or chastise X for his behaviour. By 2021/2022, she said the situation in the home had become quite tense and she began to resent the husband for his conduct, causing her to remain at the home only for the benefit of the children. She denied smacking the children but accepted that they would have seen her upset and arguing with the husband quite a lot towards the end, sometimes crying. She denied the minor allegation of having bumped a shopping trolley into X’s heels and denied any significant use of sleeping tablets (saying that she may have consumed in total two boxes of sleeping tablets over the whole of the relationship, those tablets being available for purchase over the counter without a script).
She believes that the difficulties of her relationship with X are entirely the husband's fault. This belief appears to me to be somewhat lacking in insight: it is not surprising that a teenager would react badly to discovering that a parent was having affairs, particularly in circumstances where neither parent had discussed the difficulties that confronted the family at that point with the children.
The allegations concerning the Cooking Spray Incident are a claim that she turned and sprayed cooking oil in X's eyes when he spoke to her in the kitchen. She said that X complained immediately and that the husband then stormed in. In evidence, she denied having sprayed cooking oil at X at all, however this varied slightly from her version given to the family report writer that the oil nozzle had become stuck.
The Mashed Potato Incident involved a time when she was serving mashed potato to the other family members at the table, when X was insolent, telling her that he did not “fucking want any”. This led her to throw the spoon, and some mashed potato hit X’s neck. She said she did not know that the spoon had broken. She was aware that X became upset, said she felt bad and went around to cuddle him. She denies the claim that she responded by saying “fuck this family” before going to her bedroom. She accepts that the incident would have been upsetting for X but thinks that it was built up into an incident of greater significance than it was in context. She is of the belief that the husband had done something to X to make the red mark that appears in a photograph, as in her view the mark was on X’s back and under his shirt.
She denied that she had attempted to push the husband down the stairs, but agreed that they were at times in arguments and argued when she was in bed. She knew that he had mobility difficulties but said that he could walk, but not bend or run, nor could he get up if he fell on the ground or sat down.
The wife recounted that, on occasions, the husband would recount discussions between the husband and wife to X.
She explained that an argument in City K had developed because she wanted pictures with both of the children, and X did not want pictures taken.
Towards the end of the relationship it appears that there was an upper body shot on the wife’s phone that one of the children may have seen, and she was unsure whether there may have been some conversations with respect to relationships and intimate messages. She accepted that this would have been confronting for the children. She denied taking any partial or wholly naked photos of herself in front of X.
She accepted that when supervised time was attempted at M Contact Centre in 2023, Y would not get out of the car when brought there by the husband.
When cross-examined by the ICL, the wife said that there had been no intimacy between the parties for some time and that the husband became jealous of X’s relationship with her.
The wife presented as neatly dressed and cooperative although slightly nervous witness. She was clearly concerned about the outcome of the proceedings, and very distressed about the lack of relationship with the children.
During the course of the wife giving evidence, she appeared somewhat emotionally labile, quickly becoming distressed and quickly recovering and at times engaging in significant hand gestures. When discussing the incidents with X I formed the impression that she was minimising the events to some extent. However, I also bear in mind that giving evidence in a proceeding such as this is particularly stressful, and that to date, the impact of the relationship breakdown and subsequent separation has been overwhelmingly negative for her.

The wife’s current partner

The wife’s current partner, Mr P, presented as a reliable witness who was clearly supportive of the wife and prepared to adjust his relationship with her as may be required to best enable Y to renew his relationship with the wife.
Mr P is currently living with her. He plans to return to his father’s home if the wife has Y living with her, at least for the initial period. He said that he planned to come over on weekends from around a month after any change of care arrangements and probably not see the wife during the first month. He said that they had not discussed the detail as yet. He also confirmed that he had lent $50,000 to the wife for legal expenses.

The wife’s therapist, Ms S

Ms S, a therapist on whom the wife has been attending, diagnosed the wife with PTSD. It seems she concluded the wife had an adjustment disorder on the first session, and then PTSD by the third session. The diagnosis appears to be based upon the proposition that the wife has difficulty with day-to-day functioning with respect to concentration, attention and memory. Ms S was not able to identify Criterion A factors from the DSM-5 to support the PTSD diagnosis, and said that she relied only upon traits.
She purported to venture a diagnosis for the husband, although maintained it was not an official diagnosis and that she had simply given the wife material about narcissistic personality disorder. She had never seen the husband or the children. Remarkably, she did not have all of her notes with her when she came to give evidence.
It is unfortunate that she did not undertake a thorough assessment of the wife and explore the wife’s perceptions of the family dynamics so as to be in a position to provide evidence as to the likely capacity of the wife to parent Y if he was placed in her care.
I found the wife’s therapist a less than helpful witness. I did not have the impression that she had brought a critical mind to her therapy with the wife. I found her evidence of no real assistance at all in assessing the parties or determining the issues in dispute in this case. The wife was fortunate that Dr C had spent some time interrogating the parties as to the steps they had taken to improve their parenting and that Mr FF had made some observations in this regard.

The husband

The husband was cross-examined at length. He accepted that the wife can be protective of the children. He believes that the children look up to him and see him as a role model.
He said that he cannot recall if he saw lawyers after the wife’s first affair many years ago. He said that he was not angry about her having an affair but more so “disappointed.” He denied that he had gone about building a case against the wife since 2010, although accepted that he had obtained a referral to U Mental Health Services and Professor V for both him and the wife to be assessed but that only he attended upon Professor V. Medical records from 2010 indicated that the wife had wanted another child but he was concerned about having another child due to her having had an affair. The GP’s notes from 2011 indicated the wife reporting that she was missing affection from the husband and that the husband was drinking.
Notes from 2019 indicated that the husband told the doctor that his solicitor had advised him to drag out the family law proceedings on the basis that once X is 11 or 12 he will have his say, however the husband denied deciding to drag out the proceedings, although he has made numerous application (around ten) and has not been expeditious in his compliance with some of the orders.
He started a diary in 2023, although it was not immediately discovered in the disclosure process. He said that he was advised it would be good to keep a diary of what he had done for the children and of the events that took place. He said he started it during the holiday in City K and he was not sure whether the marriage was coming to an end.
He accepted that he had told the wife that it was better that she move out of the home. He also said that he had tried to find another home, but it did not suit him.
By May 2023 he declined to allow the wife to take Y to school, which he explained on the basis that he thought that the wife was “building a platform again”, even though at this point she was still residing in the same home. He denied that the wife was cooking and making lunches, and alleged that she was lying in bed a lot during this period. He said that he made breakfast in bed for the wife a lot of the time.
He said in his affidavit that X called the wife a slut and that he did reprimand X for this and X did not use the word “slut” again after that. However, it seems clear that the husband did not attempt to contextualise with X that the relationship of the parties had broken down.
He said that X had only recorded one telephone call with the wife. He accepted that one of the diary entries set out in his affidavit was not able to be located in the original diary.
When asked about a note from the GP that referred to a mental health plan for “him”, he took the view that that was probably X that the doctor was talking about, rather than himself. He denies that he ever had depression or had been prescribed Zoloft, as alleged by the wife. There was no evidence led of him having received prescriptions for Zoloft or a diagnosis of depression.
He accepted that he had not taken the assessment as recommended by Dr C at paragraph 175 of the Single Expert Report dated 11 October 2024, which provided:
As previously discussed, it is considered essential that [the husband] be mandated to engage in structured psychological therapy with a suitably qualified and experienced male clinical or forensic psychologist, who has an understanding of working with people within conflicted family court matters, in order to assist him in addressing his unresolved emotional issues and his distorted thinking patterns regarding co-parenting and separation. [The husband] will also experience a considerable benefit from engaging fully in parenting courses as previously outlined. It is understood that he has engaged briefly in parenting courses; however, he has not attended to the information appropriately, and has received no benefit from completing these courses in an online forum. Therefore [the husband] should be mandated to attend all courses in a face to face format. Finally, it is important that [the husband] engage in the family therapy process, as previously stated this should begin with individual sessions run by an experienced family therapist, incorporating both [the husband] and [the wife], to allow them to both reach the same level of understanding of future therapy goals, through exploration and psychoeducation as to their children's needs and to ensure that they both are able to understand and implement health parenting strategies in the future.
As cross-examination continued, it became clear that he was opposed to all of Dr C’s recommendations, but had little insight into what was happening for Y, despite accepting that Y had been worse since not seeing his mother. He maintained that Y still does not want to see his mother on FaceTime. He maintained that, in his view, X was not aligned with him, but that it was X’s own voice, based on what he had experienced, which has led him to reject his mother.
Cross-examination concerning the school records and access to them did not lead to clear evidence from the husband, other than to the effect that he told X to leave the portal open to allow the wife to see school reports, however, he does not appear to have taken control of that situation.
It was put to the husband that there were records that he had told the school that the wife was not interested in the child's schooling (see Exhibit 16), however he denied this and said it must have been an error. He said that, whilst he had advised X to allow the wife to continue to access his schooling information, he did not force X to do so, phrasing it as respecting X’s decision. However, the husband agreed that he sent an email saying that he would not share X’s details (see wife’s affidavit filed 23 June 2025, pp.8–9 paragraph 45).
He accepted that he threw a bottle, as set out in the wife’s tender bundle at p.36, but maintained that it was not thrown at the wife. He said that he had no intention of causing harm to her, saying that she provoked him, before accepting that it is “probably not an appropriate thing to do”. He denied that any bruise resulted from the bottle being thrown, and denied that there was any damage to a work camera that the wife may have had possession of at the time.
The husband was critical of Ms D, reported by the GP as being behaviour that was “disgusting”. He said that he would not have used the word disgusting, but that he was unhappy. He said he had told Ms D that he did not think that Y would see the wife, and he accepts that the wife had asked about Y more than twice.
He described the Mashed Potato Incident as the wife going into a rage and belting X with a spoon. Despite this, and the alleged injuries, he did not take X to see the doctor until six days later, by which time there were no visible marks. He said that the photos he had taken were after the incident in X’s room.
During cross-examination, he set out the sequence of events as being X leaving the dinner table after being struck by the spoon, the wife then leaving, and him then placing Y in the games room before going to check on the wife. He said he then went to the shed, where X had gone, and brought X back up to his bedroom and took the photographs. He said he then went downstairs and photographed the mess and cleaned up. However, on other occasions, he described himself as having gone to the shed to check on X before going up to the see the wife.
It appears that he recorded his interaction with the wife when he went upstairs to see her, without her knowledge. He said that he did this as he was there to protect the children, saying that it was “[n]ot so much to gather evidence, but it was there to protect my children because of the violent outburst [the wife] did” (Transcript 6 August 2025, p.266 lines 21–22). It seems that he had made the wife a cup of Milo and taken it upstairs, and that he told Y (who was in bed with the wife) to come downstairs with him, rather than having sent Y to the games room before going up to check on the wife.
When he took X to the police to make a complaint, he also took Y with him.
It transpired that Y was not at school on the days of trial, as the husband was unable to get him to school whilst coming to court. It is unclear why the paternal grandmother could not have taken him to school.
After the Mashed Potato Incident, the husband kept the broken spoon in a toolbox in the shed, he said for the safety of the children. It is difficult to understand why a broken plastic spoon would have presented any risk to the children if it had been thrown away. He gave it to the police when X made the complaint. The paternal grandmother was present with X at his interview with the police.
He sent photographs of the aftermath of the Mashed Potato Incident to Mr W a friend of his, on his phone, and accepted that he had expressed a negative view of the wife around that time. He accepted that the children would have spoken to each other about the issues, and that X would have given Y an unfavourable view of the wife. The husband said that he never stepped in, but attempted to minimise this and described X as not talking much to Y about these things.
He accepted that it would be important to encourage Y to maintain a relationship with the wife, and says he would have done so, saying that he had told Y that he would be going to visits every fortnight. He did not seem able to provide any details of genuine engagement with Y concerning emotional issues that were confronting the family, and in particular, Y and his mother.
Y had said to Mr FF that the husband would be sad if he “picked” the wife’s side. Indeed, it seems that the husband gave permission to Y not to continue a relationship with the wife, telling him that no one would push him and that he might continue as long as he wished with Dr B. This was likely to leave Y in a difficult position if he thought that neither the husband nor X wanted him to see the wife. At this point, Y was only eight years of age.
The husband denies that he monitors Y’s calls with the wife. This seems inconsistent with notes in the ICL’s tender bundle, at pages 182 and 185, which form part of Exhibit 21.
Interestingly, X attended at each occasion seeing Mr FF, even though X was not required to come for visits. It is difficult to see how this shows any insight on the part of the husband, who knows that X is strongly opposed to any ongoing relationship with the wife. The husband has also been deleting text messages between him and X on the telephones, which he maintains are simply to “clean up... the phone” (Transcript 7 August 2025, p.381 line 26).

The husband’s evidence on property issues

With respect to property issues, the husband referred to a non-disclosure agreement concerning his compensation, before disclosing that the full settlement amount he received (after legal fees) was $1,034,000 in July 2023. He said he now only has $100,000 left from those monies. He had sold Water Vessel 1 and trailer (Item 9 on the balance sheet) for $25,000 in cash, even though he had purchased it for $50,000, which he explained on the basis that it was a quick sale. He said he sold it at a loss to obtain funds. Item 13, Water Vessel 2, was sold a year ago for $90,000, and Item 14, Water Vessel 3, sold for about $24,000 to $25,000.
He said that he knew he had a payment plan with the ATO for payment of tax since 2023, however he accepted that in his earlier affidavit he said that he expected to have paid his tax liabilities by March 2025.
He said that the Z Company loan in the wife’s name was for his Motor Vehicle 1.
It was put to him that he could have paid his ATO debts if he had not purchased other items, and it seems that he elected to maintain pre-separation debt on the basis that this would be more advantageous to him than paying it out.
The parties had some dispute with respect to child support, and the husband had applied for a departure concerning school fees. He said that his mother (the paternal grandmother) had gifted him $35,000 for X’s school fees (which were $12,500 per annum), but that he had spent the money on renovations. However, in his affidavit from 2023, he said that he would pay the school fees. He has received income protection insurance monies since his accident in March 2016. He says that he is not well enough to start a business again, although he is well enough to mow the lawn and do some housework (but not to kneel down). His payments from income protection are around $7,200 per month, which will continue until he is 67 years of age.
Whilst there were two defaults on the Z Company loan, he is up-to-date with the loan with respect to Motor Vehicle 1. The husband maintained that the rates debt accrued when the wife was in the home.
The husband said that his mother paid off the wife’s car for $16,000. He accepted that he incurred costs in these proceedings of $675,299.
I did not find that the husband presented as an open and frank witness. His lack of detail concerning financial issues, knowing that they were a real issue in the proceedings, also undermines his credibility. It appears that he tended to exaggerate those matters negative to the wife’s case and minimised his role in the difficulties the family faced.

Mr W

Mr W was called to give evidence for the husband’s case. He is a friend of the husband who previously worked with him. He was unable to explain why the husband has two telephones. He was not cross-examined with respect to what he said at paragraph 18 of his affidavit, being:
[The husband] and the children really enjoy camping, so after the funeral, we would often camp for the whole weekend in the bush from [City AA] and [BB River] to [Town CC] and [Town DD], giving us a chance to relax and talk openly. Whilst camping, [X] would confide in me, telling me that at times he did not want to go home as he hated walking on egg shells in that house with his mum there. I believed that [X] feared his mother. I recall telling [X] "my number is in both your dad's phones, and anytime you don't feel safe get one of your dad's phones and you can call me."
He maintains that he did not tell others how to parent, and that he thought the majority of meals were cooked by the husband.
At paragraph 19 of his affidavit, he said:
I clearly remember getting a call from [the husband] in around early 2022, saying "[the wife] has hit [X]". [The husband] sent photos of the pot with hot potato she had thrown in anger at [X] and hit him with a spoon. Then on another occasion, [the wife] lashing out at [X], spraying cooking spray directly into his face and going into his eyes. I advised [the husband] to take [X] to the family doctor on both occasions and also recommend he talk to the police and that [X] talk to a counsellor for the abuse he was copping at home from his part-time mother.
The photographs appear to have been sent by SMS on 7 April 2022 from a phone number ending in 838.
Mr W presented as a frank witness whose evidence I largely accept as reliable.

The paternal grandmother

The paternal grandmother, Mrs EE, was called to give evidence. She last spoke to the wife at the end of 2022. She said that the husband rang her about the Mashed Potato Incident because he was concerned, and that she went to the police station to “get advice”, and was told by the police that it was serious. She said that she was asked to go to the police station by the husband. She explained that the police didn’t question X at the interview, and that when X was nervous, she prompted him by asking about the events, such as “what about the pot?” She said that Y was simply playing with toys at the police station.
It is unclear why she was not able to drive Y to school on the days of trial. She said that she drives a lot and has been retired since 2018. She says she does country driving, although could drive Y to school as it is near where she does her shopping. She said she had a busy lifestyle.
She had no positive comments to make about the mother.

The therapist, Dr B

The therapist who had been working with the children, Dr B, gave evidence. She said she only saw X twice, and that X felt okay and did not need to see her anymore. This seems an odd outcome (that X did not need therapeutic assistance) given that X has clearly rejected his mother completely. It is difficult to see that a complete rejection of his mother is a sound psychological position for him to be left in for the remainder of his teenage years, and perhaps his entire life.
The therapist did not consider it appropriate to speak to the wife, and at the first appointment saw the husband together with the children. She could not recall Y having said that the husband or X might be mad if he saw the wife, but recounted that he had said that the wife might hit him hard, and that he was concerned about the Cooking Spray Incident and the Mashed Potato Incident, and the claim that the wife had attempted to hurt the husband by pushing him down the stairs.
Dr B was quite firm that the children were in need of family counselling. She agreed that she was aware of the Australian Psychological Society guidelines about seeing only one of the siblings, and said that perhaps she should have sent X to another therapist, as she then described him as a distressed and angry young man. She did not make diagnoses or provisional diagnoses, and nor did she develop a treatment plan. She said that she uses cognitive behavioural therapy and that it is difficult to determine a timeframe when improvements might be expected.
My impression of her evidence is that she approached the complex problems of this family uncritically and failed to understand the complexities of the parental conflict as a result of not seeing the wife. I am not persuaded that she conducted effective therapy, nor that she has insights upon which weight may be placed.

The family report writer

Dr C was an impressive witness in the witness box, giving clear answers with confidence.
Significantly, Dr C, in his October 2024 report stated at paragraph 128 that:
...[Y] now exhibits overwhelming distress when speaking or thinking about his mother, highlighting the deep cognitive dissonance that he experiences in actively rejecting his primary maternal caregiver. [Y’s] overwhelming distress and the flooding of emotion observed in his interview is believed to be due to the internal conflict he is experiencing between the innate belief that his mother should (and most likely did) provide him with abundant love and security and the perception that his mother has rejected this role, which he has taken as evidence of her lack of care for him. On one hand, [Y] has the instinctual need for maternal affection and security, but on the other, [the husband]’s negative narrative reinforces the idea that his mother is neglectful and unloving.
At that time Dr C thought that (at paragraph 145):
An important step in rectifying the relationships in this family is for [Y] to be able to spend extensive periods of quality time in his mother's care. This will enable him to begin to rebuild an independent relationship with her that does not involve [the husband] or [X] and is not subject to his constraining, if not discouraging, influence.
However, the context of the difficulties is identified (at paragraphs 148 and 211) where he says:
As such, and when considering the narratives of the children, all of whom have been made privy to their mother’s relationships with other men, [the husband]’s intentions appear to be somewhat self-focused and questions need to be asked in relation to his motivations. It is apparent that he has engaged in the subjugation of his entire family, working specifically to achieve the alignment of the children with his views. But of concern is that he has done so with particular manipulative skill and continues to do so at the present time.
...
To date, [the husband] has shown a persistent refusal to facilitate a healthy and ongoing relationship between the children and [the wife], indicating that he is not actively supporting the children's connection to her. His behaviour has indicated a lack of willingness to prioritise the children's need for balanced relationships with both parents, which is essential for their emotional and psychological development, over that of his own.
In his July 2025 Addendum Report, Dr C considered the changes each parent had made by engaging in therapy and courses, saying:
In the interviews for the SER, [the wife] was able to detail her engagement in these programs and in her therapy and provide insights into how she has worked to improve her parental capacity. These were recommendations which were mentioned in the CIR, and reflects on her willingness to accept help and be insightful about working to improve her parenting capacity. It is recommended still, in light of the recent criminal proceedings judgement, that [the wife] should continue to see a psychologist (presuming that practitioner has experience in conflicted family court matters) to help her better navigate the difficult issues rising from these findings with her children if/when they bring this up in the future, which is most probable.
Regrettably, the same cannot be said about [the husband] however, indeed to the contrary. The same recommendations were provided for him in the CIR, and in the SER, for him to engage in similar therapeutic supports and programs for parents. Whilst [the husband] reported completing a parenting course in the SER, he could not recall the name of the course nor his retained learning, rather he had stated, ‘I learnt nothing. Everything was things I have already done’. It continues to be this clinician’s perspective, that if not already ordered, that [the husband] be mandated to attend face-to-face, group-based parenting courses (in person or via AVL), within a specified time frame, to ensure that he attends the courses as requested and engages with the content provided by these courses in an effort to improve his level of insight as to what is in the best immediate and long-term interests of his children.
(Italics in original)
Dr C set out clearly that some changes were needed, saying:
At this stage in time, arguably, if there is not a demonstrable change of heart and effort by the father, there is increasing benefit for [Y] being removed from this environment and put back into the care of his mother. Because if his father is not prepared to accept help (and engage with same) to reunite [Y] with his mother, then it is reasonable to assume that there will be no change to the current situation. This has been covered at length in the SER, and in the subsequent questions posed by both parties, and these views continue to hold true taking into account the additional information received.
In that report Dr C concluded:
Hence at the point, mindful of the prolonged and extremely conflicted nature of these proceedings, the options remain stark for the Court to consider. Option one would be for the Court to permit the current care arrangements remain in place, thereby condoning the circumstances in which the younger child’s distorted view has developed and consolidated over time, to continue, thereby crystallising his estrangement and persisting loss of his maternal figure. The repercussions of this loss on any child’s development are not just considered profound, but lifelong.
The second option, taking into account the ramifications of the previous option, is to implement a radical reversal of care arrangements for this child, so that an opportunity is provided for [Y] and his mother to repair their fractured relationship without the negative influence of the father. It is readily acknowledged that implementing such an option is not without short-term distress and disruption for the child, but when balanced against the enormity of loss associated with maternal absence for the remainder of his childhood and adolescent development that his current trajectory predicates, then this choice becomes far more tolerable.
If by the time of the Court hearing, there has been little or no progress made in the reunification efforts (between [Y] and his mother) by the Family Therapist, as per Orders of 30 June 2025, then it may leave the Court with little option other than to weigh heavily the mother’s proposal for a radical reversal of care and a non-contact period between [Y] and his father (and potentially [X]) for a minimum of three months, though possibly extending to six months.
(Citation omitted)
Dr C gave evidence and was cross-examined, in addition to the significant evidence in his reports. He commended Mr FF’s efforts, noting that Mr FF had brought Y to the doorway of the room, which was further than he had reached in trying to have Y see the wife.
Dr C was concerned about the husband and X being in the waiting room when the sessions with Mr FF were to take place, explaining that it is a difficult situation for a small boy like Y, who he expects would have perceived the husband and X marshalled against the wife, sitting waiting in the rooms for him to finish. Dr C believes that one can be confident that it is unlikely Y and his mother will be able to resolve their relationship issues in a setting where the husband and X were present. He was unsurprised that, in those circumstances, little or no progress was made. He viewed this as evidence for why a no-contact period would be important if change of care arrangements were made for Y.
Dr C explained that the primary objective was to bring together Y and his mother so as to enable Y to reconcile his relationship with his mother. He is of the view that reversing care is an absolute last resort, and had spoken to the husband about this critical issue. It surprised him that minimal gains had been made to date.
In his view, Y's fear of his mother is a combination of sadness and loss, and Y has likely experienced a profound loss over the last two years with his mother not being part of his life. He believes that the coping mechanism Y relies upon is avoidance. He sees the loss as crystallised through the ongoing factors of living with the husband and X, and their clear negative views of the wife.
He noted that Y is of an age where he is likely to be relatively compliant, and whilst some attention may be necessary for the possibility of him absconding, he did not see this as a significant risk. He did believe that the wife would need a seasoned clinician to assist her in managing Y at the beginning, and that a holiday at the outset would be a good idea.
Dr C thought that a three to six-month moratorium would be appropriate, before reintegration with the husband and X. In order to encourage proper compliance, he thought it appropriate that a moratorium clock could be reset if Y were to abscond. He believed that the reunification of X and Y ought to be supervised by Mr FF, followed by supervised time with the husband.
Dr C was asked about the possibility that any change of care might result in significant harm to the relationship between Y, and his father and brother. Dr C was of the view that there was no chance of exclusion of Y by his father or X, as it would not be Y’s choice to live with his mother, but a choice by the court. He noted that Y’s anxiety was currently at such levels that it even impacted his speech, and that he is likely to suffer significant panic if told that his living arrangements could be changed, however Dr C believed that this would pass. Dr C reflected on the potential risks to Y of any change of care, noting that he is an anxious child and not an angry externaliser. Dr C was of the view that absconding was a low risk, and he reminded those present that the wife’s sensitivity to Y would be essential, and that it would be necessary to be mindful of the potential of self-harm as the child may well have a tendency towards internalising behaviours.
Dr C believed that the possibility of reconciliation between X and his mother has now passed, and that the window of opportunity to make changes to the relationship between Y and his mother is narrowing. He believed the rupture to Y’s relationship with his mother remains a result of the events over the last couple of years and is profoundly damaging to Y, as the risk of no action will be a probable loss of relationship entirely with his mother, and the continuation for Y of life in a deep and fractured family system. This has to be seen in the context of Dr C’s view that the husband is rigid and intolerant, and why Dr C has suggested that therapy for the husband would be of some assistance, as the husband has not taken ownership of any aspect of the relationship failure.
He believes that the wife has reflected upon her own conduct and manages her own traits more successfully. He noted that Mr FF had observed her as a calmer and more positive person in the sessions with him. He believed that the wife’s continuation with the reunification counselling, despite the exceedingly slow progress, was both empathetic and insightful.
In cross-examination he was asked about the possibility that a change of care would be profoundly rupturing of the relationship between the husband and X, but maintained that he did not have any sense that the husband would walk away from Y. Rather, Y is currently caught within the conflict between the parents with fear in transitions. He acknowledged the significance of the incidents that had occurred, but was clear that in his view they were not of such gravity as to result in the termination of a relationship between a child and a parent. Rather, the husband could have been the “wise elephant in the room”, rather than allowing the situation to develop as it has.
Dr C accepted that the husband’s actions may well have been subconscious rather than a conscious course, set out to align X to him and cause the wife to become estranged from the children. He accepted that the estrangement would, in part, be due to the experiences the children have had with their mother, but did not consider them to be the significant motivator for the estrangement. He noted that Y’s reasons, even years later, do not have a realistic basis for the behaviours that he is displaying.
He found it perplexing that the husband put forward proposed orders for steps that he could have been doing for years, and as a result did not think that the husband’s heart and soul was in the success of any family therapy.
He made even the simple observation that the husband could have left X at home and sought earliest appointments for family therapy if he had been committed to the success of that family therapy. He was doubtful that family therapy would ever be successful while the current care arrangements continued.

The family therapist, Mr FF

Mr FF provided a written report outlining his attempts to have Y commence seeing his mother. Unfortunately, he got no further than having Y sit in the doorway when his mother was in the room. Whilst not expressing opinions, some of his observations are telling:
In a follow-up individual session with [Y] following the failed first attempted joint session, [Y] explored factors that may have contributed to his decision not to enter the therapy room with his mother. [Y] stated that he thought his father and brother might be feeling disappointed, mad or sad about him seeing his mother. He continued by saying that he would feel disappointed in himself if he let his father down and said, “I’m not going to do that, because I’m not going to see her”. [Y] was encouraged by the therapist to engage in the next joint session with his mother.
...
[The husband] spoke positively about [Y] and reported that he was doing well at school and had received positive feedback from teachers. The father proudly stated that he’d “taken them ([X] and [Y]) away from their mother and that other guy and put them in a better place”.

...
At the conclusion of [Y’s] appointment on 3 July 2025, the therapist met briefly with [the husband] and [X], in hope that they could be supported by the therapist to in turn support [Y] to engage in the next joint session with his mother. It was communicated that the therapist opined that with encouragement from both family members, [Y] could feel reassured. Furthermore, it was relayed to them that [Y] had reported to the therapist that a barrier to progressing to joint sessions was based on how he perceived [the husband] and [X] might feel about the reconnection. Furthermore, it was acknowledged that while they might have felt an instinct to protect [Y], the therapist would be present to guide the session and future sessions for [Y].
[X] raised that he did not want to see his mother in the waiting room when [Y] was to meet with [the wife]. The therapist advised that the Practice staff do their best to utilise two waiting areas, however in an abundance of caution, [X] need not attend the sessions.
[X] accompanied [Y] and his father to each session. [Y] was consistently polite. He warmly greeted and saluted the therapist, by name and with a firm handshake each time.

...
In my view, [Y] has taken on a protective role within his father’s household, at the cost of his own emotional clarity. When he sees his mother, he appears overwhelmed, not necessarily because he feels unsafe, but perhaps because he feels the grief from the cut off. [Y] may be mistakenly attributing his grief to [the wife] being a harmful person, which teaches his brain that emotional discomfort must be caused by her, rather than recognising that it is a normal response to sadness, loss or loyalty conflict.
It is recommended that therapy continue and all family members remain engaged in the therapeutic process to support [Y’s] emotional wellbeing and promote the possibility of future repair with his mother...
Mr FF was called to give evidence. He was concerned not to express views about the parties as he wished to preserve the extent of the therapeutic relationship that he had established. The parties chose to respect this view.
He explained that he had spoken to the husband about preparing Y for sessions, and in an initial session had noted that the husband’s eyes were watery and flushed, that he was a bit upset, and that he had told the husband that it was important that he quarantined his emotions.
X attended on every occasion. Although he was always polite and well-mannered, Mr FF thought that his presence could have added a layer of stress for Y. He explained that he would have preferred more time to work with Y and would be prepared to continue to work with the family. He had another appointment with the husband in a couple of weeks’ time.
When he was asked to consider the three options that were, in substance, open to the court (formal therapy, reversal of care, or a cessation of interventions), he reiterated the importance of him remaining neutral as a therapist who may continue to work with the family. However, he did express the view that he was not sure that more time would assist.
He said that if Y were to remain with the husband, it would be helpful for Y to understand attachments better and become more ambivalent in his thinking, saying it often takes a long time and needs to be done before joint sessions. He was also of the view that the husband needed coaching to support Y and to quarantine his own emotions. The wife needed to be supported to ensure that she did not pressure Y.
Mr FF expressed the view that if Y were to be residing with the wife, immediate and intense therapy would be required to adjust his understanding and the inevitable grief that he would feel as a result of a change in the care. Mr FF was an impressive witness.

PARENTING ISSUES

X will soon be an adult and does not wish to have a relationship with his mother. Having regard to his age no orders were sought with respect to his parenting, although the wife remains hopeful that she may one day have a relationship with him.
Y is still of an age where mending his relationship with the wife remains a real possibility, and of importance to Y if a relationship can be renewed. Accordingly, the parenting issues in this case only concern Y (“the child”).

Wife’s relationship with her family

There was much dispute as to how the wife’s relationship with her family had broken down. The wife alleges that the husband pressed her to cut off her family and the husband alleges that her father (the maternal grandfather) was controlling and pressuring her to contact her own mother more often. An AVO was issued against the maternal grandfather in protection of the wife in 2009 for a duration of twelve months. Further the maternal grandfather filed proceedings to recover money from the husband, although his claim was dismissed. The wife’s evidence on this issue was reticent and did not appear to be frank and open. However, the difficult relationship between the parties and the wife’s parents is not of great significance in these proceedings.

Washing Liquid Incident

Both the husband and the wife gave evidence as to this incident. There was no evidence aside from their versions. Having seen both give evidence, I accept the wife’s evidence that the husband threw a bottle of washing liquid at her resulting in bruising.

Cooking Spray Incident

The wife says that in 2022, X called out “Dad. Mum’s just sprayed oil at me.” The wife says that she was in the kitchen preparing dinner when X came running in to get a snack before going back to the games room whilst calling out to his father. The wife denies spraying cooking oil at him and said that when she looked at X, he was smiling. The wife says that the husband then said loudly to her “What the fuck do you think you are doing?”, and that she replied that “[X] just deliberately made that up”.
The husband says that the wife sprayed cooking oil “directly into [X’s] eyes”.
The incident is alleged to have occurred around a year before the report was made to the police (after the parties had separated).
Whilst giving oral evidence, the wife stated:
...At the time, it was only [X] and I in the house. [X] was playing his PlayStation on the weekend in the afternoon. I started to cook dinner. I had the fry pan. I sprayed the oil on the fry pan. [X] just came running out of the games room to quickly get something from the cupboard, like a packet of chips, then ran back. That was the end of that episode, but then saw [the husband] just walking back from the shed to come and see [X] because he wouldn’t allow [X] to be without him for prolonged period of times, and then [X] thought it was funny to say, “Dad, mum just sprayed oil in my face”, and then [the husband] storms in and says, “Why did you do that for?” and I said, “Well, why – like why are you getting upset with me? Like, nothing happened. [X] is just saying that”, and then that – that then became a big ordeal once I left a year later.

(Transcript 4 August 2025, p.37, lines 30–41)
When questioned about what she had told the single expert report writer at interviews regarding an oil nozzle becoming stuck, the wife stated:
The oil lid, the spray bottle, as in, the – the spray bottle, as in, it wasn’t spraying, but then I had to lift it up, but [X] was metres away. It didn’t direct at him. He wasn’t near. He didn’t stop for a minute. Even in the kitchen, he just came, grabbed his packet of chips and then left...

There was no spraying at [X]. I was just spraying with the bottle, cooking dinner, and [the husband] is just trying to think of scenarios to put in his affidavit, and he remembers that episode of [X] saying, “Dad” – when [the husband] knew nothing had happened and would never happen, but he liked to, in front of [X], get angry at me to show that his – supporting [X]. It was a very unusual dynamic.

(Transcript 4 August 2025, p.38, lines 16–27)
This extra detail about the wife holding a spray bottle at the time of this alleged incident was not in her affidavit.
In the appeal judgment from the District Court, the judge notes that this incident wasn’t recorded in X’s diary and that X had said “I can’t remember the whole thing exactly” and that “[the wife] would have turned round or she would have been walked, like, walking-walking, like, not walking but, like, turned around at me to get something”.
Dr C, in his October 2024 report noted that X had written a letter to the ICL and rewritten it to read to the report writer (at paragraph 103) and said that:
[X’s] letter contained information about his experience of being cross examined at his mother’s criminal trial after she had sprayed cooking oil into his eyes during an argument and hit him with a potato masher, ‘how can she put her child through a one-and-a-half-hour cross examination. Why wouldn't she just admit to it?’. [X] then raised that his mother had been in Melbourne having an affair with another man while his paternal grandfather was in hospital.
Comment: [X’s] narrative appeared identical to that of his father, matching in both incident and wording. Overall it provided a very strong indication that [the husband] has been involving [X] in matters relating to his mother and likely adversely impacting [X’s] view of his mother.
...
[X] expressed his view that he believed his mother only made telephone calls [to] himself and [Y] because ‘she has to’. He stated ‘she doesn't care about me. You don't abuse your child and spray oil in their face’. [X] acknowledged that his father sometimes gets cross with him, and will raise his voice, ‘nowhere near as much as Mum’. [X] stated that he wanted nothing to do with his mother for the rest of his life because he did not believe that she cared about him, ‘anyone who does that to their child can't like them’. When asked to consider the possibility that he may be wrong in his belief about his mother’s feelings towards him, [X] replied ‘she doesn't care about me. It's not possible. She doesn't. I will never forget or forgive her’.
The repeated use of the third person when recounting the incidents (“her child”, “your child”, “their child”) exhibits a level of detachment that seems surprising if X is describing events that distressed him to the extent he made a complaint to police.
The husband’s affidavit only contained a brief statement about the incident, saying (at paragraph 52): “On another instance at home, [the wife] sprayed cooking oil directly into X’s eyes. She never felt sorry for him or apologised for her actions, instead blaming him or me.” He was not cross‑examined on his memory of this event. He was asked about the reasons he believed Y was unsafe with the wife, to which he stated his belief was based on “her past actions with the violence in the family”, being “the injuries to [X] and what she has done to him and different things, which [Y] has been around. I don’t want that happening to [Y]” (the incident with the oil spray and the incident with the potato being two of the incidents) (Transcript 5 August 2025, p.168, lines 6–24).
I am not persuaded that the incident occurred as alleged by the husband and X. I do not accept that the wife actually sprayed cooking oil into X’s eyes.

Mashed Potato Incident
