In April 2022, the wife was serving mashed potato to the husband and children at the dinner table.
The wife says that she asked X whether he wanted gravy with the mashed potato and that X responded “I don't want fucking mash potatoes” (mother’s affidavit filed 23 June 2025, paragraph 54(c)) or “I don’t fucking want any mashed potato” (Transcript 4 August 2025, p.40, lines 39–40). The wife says that upon hearing that response she was upset (but did not swear back at X) and threw the plastic spoon she was using to serve the mashed potatoes down and that the spoon may have hit X, but that she did not observe any marks on X.
In oral evidence, she denied hitting X but said “I got upset, I threw the spoon and I believe some mash potato went on his neck. And it was some degree hot because it was still in the pot as I was mashing it” (Transcript 4 August 2025, p.41, lines 6–9).
Later the following evidence was given (Transcript 4 August 2025, pp. 41–42):
So, on your version, he had ended up with some hot potato on him?---Yes, which –which I was – we were facing each other at the time, but then, after dinner, they went down to the shed, which is what – where I had my concerns about.

So you think, do you, that [Mr Abraham] did something to [X] - - -?---Yes.

- - - to make the red marks?---Yes, to take the photo because he knew that I was – it was inevitable and imminent that I was leaving him.

So that he deliberately injured [X], or do you think it’s make-up?---I don’t know injured, but I – to make a red mark, yes. There’s no other way that could have happened.

Well, you don’t think hot mashed potato might do that?---It’s – it’s – it’s on the back of him and under his shirt, like – where’s the other picture?
The husband recounts this incident differently, stating in his affidavit:
In April 2022, we were having dinner one night when [the wife] asked [X] if he wanted mashed potato. [X] said no and [the wife] insisted. The situation escalated with [X] swearing at [the wife] and [the wife], smacking him in the neck and shoulder with a hot spoon containing the mashed potato with such force that it broke into pieces and burnt him. [The wife] continued to throw the hot saucepan containing the food at the floor.
The husband said in oral evidence that the wife went into “an absolute rage and belted [X]” and “she went into a rage and in a couple split second just went bang, bang pot and then broken spoon on [X]. And it’s a plastic spoon so it’s a lot of force” (Transcript 6 August 2025, p.223, lines 10–12). The husband says that the force of the wife’s smacks to X’s neck and shoulders broke the spoon into pieces and left burn marks on X (the wife says she only found out the spoon was broken afterwards, following dinner: Transcript 4 August 2025, p.41, lines 11–13). The husband says that following this incident, X told him that he no longer wanted to live with his mother and wanted nothing more to do with her (husband’s affidavit filed 16 July 2025, paragraph 60).
There is a photograph of a mild red mark on the side of X’s neck that has the shape of a horizontal oblong area and a photo of his shoulder that has a large mildly red mark around it. The red marks do not appear to be the type or shape one would expect from being forcibly struck by a serving spoon. They appear more consistent with having been in contact with mashed potato from the pot. The serving spoon may have made contact however I am not persuaded that it did so in a way that the force of the spoon (rather than the heat of the mashed potato) caused any red mark or injury.
Whilst giving evidence, the husband recalled:
I’ve told [the wife] to go and apologise to [X], and she refused. And then she said, “he can’t swear at me.” I said, “well you’ve just belted him, you know, with the hot potato and stuff.” And she laughed it off and said, “well he can look after himself.” I said, “you need to go and apologise to your son.” She never did and never has to date.

(Transcript 6 August 2025, p.229, lines 21–27)
The husband explained the timeline of the conduct after the event in detail (Transcript 6 August 2025, pp.228 line 30 to p.229 line 27) as X going to the shed, he settling Y in the games room to watch TV, him going to see X in the shed and bringing him back to the house, and then going upstairs to see the wife, then seeing X, then photographing the mess before cleaning it up. He then changed the sequence saying he saw the wife before he went to the shed to see X (Transcript 6 August 2025, p.229 lines 39–43).
On the recording that the husband took of his conversation with the wife after the incident he tells Y to come downstairs to watch TV: clearly Y was upstairs with the wife after the incident. Whilst, at one level, it is a simple error in sequencing, it also shows that Y’s relationship with the wife was such that he went to her room with her after the incident and only left when encouraged to do so by the husband. More significantly, the husband describes the incident (in this conversation that he was recording without the wife’s knowledge) when saying to the wife “Can you please apologise to [X] for throwing potato in his face?” The wife objects, saying that X “keeps swearing at me. He’s swearing at me more and more”. Later the husband says, whilst continuing to persuade the wife to apologise, that X’s “just had, you just threw potato at his face” to which she laughs and he says “Don’t worry. It’s a bit of a shock”. There is no mention (express or implied) of X being struck by the wife, indeed the comment “Don’t worry. It’s a bit of a shock” clearly implies that there was no injury, nor that X was struck by the spoon.
X has given slightly different versions of the incident, as recounted by the District Court Judge:
When one turns first to the incident referred to as the "potato incident", this is an allegation in which the complainant asserts that he was hit with a big black plastic spoon full of potato. In his diary, he asserted that he was belted with it on his right shoulder. Fifteen days later, he gave another version which was that he was hit on the right shoulder with a spoon that left a large red mark, and then, the appellant threw it and hot potato at him which scalded the left side of his neck.
I accept that X provoked an outburst from the wife, which resulted in her throwing the pot of mashed potato onto the floor creating a mess. I also accept that some potato may have landed on X. I am not persuaded that she struck him with the spoon.
X’s behaviour that evening was insolent. It appears his behaviour in this regard had been developing for some time; the husband had done nothing to check the teenager’s disrespectful behaviours toward the wife. X provoked a response from the wife that shocked him and no doubt wounded his pride; as the husband put it, he got a bit of a fright. The wife lost her temper in response to his provocation. I am mindful that these events take place in a short period of time yet are later analysed in detail, and that few parents have had no arguments with a teenage child as an incident of parenting.
I am not persuaded that this incident indicates that the wife is a risk to the children or is a poor parent. Parents are not always perfect; children are not always respectful. When viewed through the lens of family life, the incident shows that the wife had an understandable emotive outburst and that the husband failed to assist in placing appropriate boundaries around X’s disrespectful behaviours. Had the husband been more focused upon parenting than gathering evidence, he would have intervened earlier to chastise X for his behaviour.

Pushing the husband down the stairs allegation

The claim that the wife had attempted to push the husband down the stairs became an issue of significance at trial. The husband said in his affidavit that:
In 2022, [the wife] also attempted to push me down the stairs of our two-story home on a few occasions, knowing that I had balancing issues due to my permanently injured knee. Each time, this happened, the children witnessed this, with [X] intervening to push [the wife] off me so that I wouldn't fall.
The wife denies that she attempted push the husband down the stairs, saying that when they argued upstairs she was always in bed (wife’s affidavit filed 23 June 2025, page 44 paragraph 372).
The wife denied the allegation in cross-examination, pointing out that the husband was stable on his feet (not using crutches) but restricted such that he couldn’t get up off the ground or run.
X’s version is carefully discussed in the transcript from the District Court appeal hearing (Exhibit 22), highlighting the weaknesses in his version (he was at the bottom of the stairs; the husband was facing him and on the stairs; and there was no evidence that the husband fell or stumbled; at one point saying the wife only went to push him but “didn’t push him”). X also said that he ran up and pushed the wife against a wardrobe which appears inconsistent with the alleged attempt to push the husband which was alleged to have happened on the stairs. Whilst I do not formally adopt the findings of the District Court, the arguments made in that Court highlight the very real weaknesses in X’s evidence.
I am not persuaded that the wife attempted to push the husband down the stairs or ever acted in a way that would indicate she intended to push him down the stairs.

General conduct of husband

More generally, the wife sets out a long history of the husband effectively excluding her from the lives of the children, which became quite extreme after separation when the husband would intervene to stop her taking Y to school or participating in his life. I prefer the evidence of the wife on these issues.
The parties had a fractious marriage that was increasingly unhappy over many years. Their emotional connection had withered years ago. I accept that they had a marriage punctuated by disputes.

Children’s discovery of wife’s relationships with others

Some aspects of the hearing descended into recounting the difficulties in the relationship of the parties and the wife’s steps to find a new partner. Relationships end as people grow apart. In this case there were difficulties in the parties’ relationship from the earliest days. The pressures on the relationship and family life resulting from the injuries suffered by the husband would not have helped their situation.
The extent of the material seen by the children on the mother’s phone is unclear. She was using a dating app and there is a photo produced where a male friend from running is kissing her on the cheek. X describes lewd texts and images, yet none have been produced; this may be simply because they were on the wife’s phone, but it is odd that such images or texts would not have been captured in some form, given that level of evidence gathering that had been engendered later in the relationship.
There are two factors that appear to be of real significance in the current proceedings. First, the breakdown of the parties’ relationship over such a lengthy period would have created a tense and difficult environment for everyone in the home. It would have been far better, in retrospect, had the parties separated much earlier. Secondly, the wife’s development of a separate life outside the home may well have contributed to the alignment of X with the husband. It was most unfortunate that the children learned of the wife looking for another relationship before their parents separated. The parties must both share some responsibility for not resolving their relationship difficulties and providing clear messages to the children, rather than allowing the difficulties to continue for many years resulting in pressure for the children to choose one parent or the other.

Husband’s recounting of narratives to family GP

Considerable evidence was provided describing what the husband recounted to his doctor, Dr B. Dr B was not called. Her notes are careful, identifying what she was told and what she observed. My impression of the notes is that the husband was in large part recounting self-serving versions of events to Dr B in order to generate a trail of evidence should the time come that the parties separated.

Wife’s parenting capacity

The wife has made preparations and taken steps to be able to support Y in anticipation of orders that Y live or spend time with her.
The wife has relocated to provide a better environment for Y. Her new home is in a family-orientated suburb, close to a sporting oval and with many bike paths available to support Y’s extracurricular interests and, most importantly, the new residence is close to Y’s school. The new home has four bedrooms, which would afford Y necessary privacy as he grows into a teenager, and a large backyard.
The wife has re-partnered since the separation and now lives with her new partner, Mr P. The wife and her new partner have discussed Mr P moving out of their shared home if the wife receives custody of Y to allow the wife and Y some time to revive their relationship. The wife’s current partner has given evidence he would leave the wife’s home, where he currently resides, for at least a month to allow time for Y to adjust to the new environment (Transcript 4 August 2025, p.60 line 40 to p.61 line 9). He understands that his introduction to Y would be gradual and only take place once the child is settled at the wife’s home.
The wife has indicated she understands that a moratorium period with Y would be difficult. She has arranged with her supervisors at work to take four weeks leave should she receive custody of Y.
The wife has made progress in her therapy as acknowledged by Mr FF in his report from 30 July 2025 at paragraph 37, which provided:
[The wife] appeared to take on opinions or advice suggested by the therapist. She acknowledged that at times she felt helpless. She showed insight into how these feelings had led to her being more likely to make comments to [Y] that placed him in a difficult position.
Further, external experts such as Mr FF and Dr C, have indicated they would support the wife while she repaired her relationship with Y.
The evidence indicates the children have a positive sibling relationship with one another. However, the children are not close in age, being born seven years apart from one another. X would have been able to obtain his driver’s licence in late 2025 (Transcript 4 August 2025, p.11 lines 1–6) and begin an apprenticeship soon after. This means, regardless of any court orders, the children would be spending significantly less time with each other, so the moratorium period’s effect on the children’s relationship would be minimal.
It would be necessary to include X amongst those forbidden to contact Y during the moratorium period because he impairs Y’s relationship with their mother. An example of this is at paragraph 19 of Dr C’s July 2025 Addendum Report, which provided:
...As currently, the mother has made all appropriate efforts and followed all guidelines to have contact with her children, as noted in materials that she has made efforts to contact [Y’s] treating psychologist, find out information from the school, and engage in regular phone calls, with the latter being limited by the paternal surveillance and/or [X’s] presence in these calls with [Y].
And again at paragraph 30, which explains:
...In [X’s] case however, noting his ongoing alignment with his father, and the likely ramifications on him arising from proceeding with a police report, presumably at the behest of his father more than a year after the event, ... will no doubt have further damaged an already fractured relationship with his mother to the point where possible reconciliation will be unlikely for some years to come.
The wife outlines in her affidavit that prior to separation, her and Y were “extremely close”, for example opening the gate for her on arrival home from work and giving her a hug, helping prepare dinner with her, playing games or watching television in bed together after dinner, riding their bikes together, and playing Lego (see paragraph 39). This was not disputed by the husband.
The parties dispute the extent to which the wife assisted with the children when working from home during COVID.

Ongoing family therapy

The ICL proposes that there be ongoing family therapy for Y whether there is a change of primary care or not. I am mindful that if Y remains with the husband there is nothing that appears to have changed that would give any real confidence that ongoing family therapy would have any success, given that it appears to have been entirely unsuccessful until Mr FF became involved, and that any advances with Mr FF have been minimal.
If Y’s care is not shifted to the wife, he will continue to be exposed to the negative views of the husband and X. Without changing Y’s care, I see no real prospect that he will have a relationship with his mother, despite what appears to have been a close and warm relationship prior to separation. I accept that it is likely that Y feels unable to have a relationship with his mother at present due to the impact of X and his father’s views.

The potential impact of changed care for Y

There is no doubt that Y, who has not been prepared to be in a room with the wife in the last two years (even with the assistance of therapists), would suffer acute distress in the event that orders are made changing his primary care. I am mindful that this will be a psychological harm for Y in the short term. Against this short-term harm, I must consider the potential long-term benefit of renewing his relationship with his mother.
Dr C is confident that a change of care will not fracture Y’s relationship with X or his father. That is, Dr C does not think that they would abandon or reject Y. I accept Dr C’s evidence on this point. A change of primary care therefore offers the prospect of the child having a relationship with both parents and his brother.
I accept that a change of care is the only realistic path forward for Y to have a relationship with both parents.
A change of primary care is a significant relationship change and there is no guarantee that Y will settle in his mother’s home, nor that he would build a strong attachment to her to replace the severing (at least in the short term) of his relationship with the husband. Dr C’s opinion is that a change in care is more likely than not to succeed. However, Dr C sees the prospect of Y attempting to run away as a real possibility, which would place him at risk if it occurred. That risk is mitigated a little by the wife’s proposal to take Y away for a holiday from the time of the orders so as to have a break in routines and time to re-bond. I accept that the wife is aware of the challenges she may face in parenting him at first (although like most parents it is difficulty to appreciate the full depth of those difficulties until they are experienced).
Mr FF and Dr C are both available to provide support for the parents following the orders made by the court. Mr FF has maintained his independence as a therapist, avoiding providing a critique of the parents, leaving him well placed to work with the parents and Y.
The Reasons for Judgment in the matter have not been delivered at a time so close to holidays that they would not be expected to be available.
I am comfortably satisfied that the prospects of a change of care resulting in a successful outcome for Y are good, and far outweigh the chances that a change will fail and outweigh the potential immediate adverse impacts on Y (whilst it is possible that the impact of orders changing care will immediately free Y from severe cognitive dissonance, it is appropriate that I carefully consider the potentially negative impacts).

CONCLUSION ON PARENTING ISSUES

Section 65D of the Family Law Act provides that a court may “make such parenting order as it thinks proper”. Section 60CA and s 65AA require the court to “regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration” when exercising the broad discretion provided in s 65D.
The various factors the court must consider when determining a child’s best interests are set out in s 60CC(2):
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:

(i) the child; and

(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);

(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

There has been a number of Apprehended Violence Orders as well as the criminal prosecution and the appeal from the magistrate’s findings. I have had regard to these as required by s 60CC(2A) and considered the significant incidents in detail above. I am not persuaded that Y is at risk of physical harm in the care of either parent. The real risk to Y is the psychological impact of living in the husband’s household where the husband’s views (reinforced by X) have left Y unable to have a relationship with his mother. I do not accept that this arose simply from the wife’s conduct in the relationship, given her close relationship with Y prior to separation. The situation is likely to cause long term harm in Y’s development.
As discussed above there are risks in ordering a change in Y’s care arrangements, as the change cannot be guaranteed to succeed. Whilst it is possible that upon being told of orders requiring Y live with his mother he will experience relief, it seems that there is a greater chance that he will suffer significant distress and grief for a short period.
I take into account Y’s expressed views as have been recounted by many witnesses in the hearing. The weight to be placed upon those expressions of his views must be considered in the context of the circumstances of the case. I am not persuaded that his current views and reactions are a result of his lived experience with the wife, nor free choices that he has made. I am persuaded that he feels unable to have a relationship with the wife due to the attitude of the father and X. Y’s young age also makes him more susceptible to these pressures.
I note Y’s age, and the history of interventions in this case. If I were not to order a change of care I would seriously consider ending time between Y and his mother, simply to allow him to be free of the conflict and likely ongoing sense of pressure to reject his mother. However, this would result in the loss of his mother from his life.
The capacity of the parents is discussed above. I accept that both have deficits in their parenting capacity. The husband’s passivity during the Mashed Potato Incident demonstrates a real lack of parenting capacity on his part in a situation where X had been so rude to the wife after she had cooked the family a meal.
It is clear that the wife has made significant changes since separation, whilst the husband has remained resistant (at least in the sense of passive resistance) to Y having any relationship with the wife. Whilst the husband says that he encourages a relationship I do not accept that this describes Y’s experience of life in the household. Y is also likely to suffer significant pressure from X; one only has to consider why X would even attend the sessions with Mr FF, let alone claim to be fearful in Mr FF’s rooms (discussed above) to see the level of indirect pressure placed upon Y to reject his mother.
There is a real benefit to Y having a relationship with all of his family members. I am satisfied that the chance of him having a relationship with the wife, if he remains living with the husband, is negligible. I accept Dr C’s view that the husband and X are unlikely to reject him if he lives with the wife. The impact upon a child of growing up hating a parent is a terrible thing, even where there is good reason for the animus. Here there is no satisfactory reason for Y to reject his mother.
Taking into account the lengthy discussions set out above I am persuaded that orders ought to be made for Y to live with the wife.
The changeover of care will be ordered to take place in the secure Court Child Expert rooms at the court where a Court Child Expert will be available and the ICL can explain the orders and reasons for them to Y. This will afford Y a safe place within which to process the decision and some time to overcome any initial distress that he may feel without pressure to react in any particular way.
Dr C suggests a moratorium of contact with the husband and X for three to six months. I note that this is a significant period of time for a child of Y’s age. Having regard to the circumstances of this case, and the pressure that Y no doubt feels to reject his mother, a significant period is required to allow him to establish a relationship with sufficient strength and security to withstand the pressures (at least indirect) of the husband and X. It is also important to ensure that the change of care be given the best circumstances to succeed given the likely distress Y will experience initially.
I am persuaded that a six month moratorium on direct contact is in Y’s best interests, however that after four months the husband should be permitted telephone contact once per week, supervised by a professional supervisor.
I am not persuaded that it is appropriate to order that the husband attend therapy as it will be of no use if he does not choose to approach it for the purpose of making changes. I accept that the wife will continue to obtain support from Mr FF without the necessity of an order. I am satisfied that the wife will not hold Y in therapy any longer than necessary to allow him to come to terms with his circumstances and the conduct of the adults in his life. If orders were made for therapy for a set period, that period may be too short or too long for what Y requires: that is, he primarily requires the opportunity to be a child and not pressured by the conflict of his parents or endless therapy sessions.
I am not persuaded that the very restrictive orders sought by the wife are necessary in the context of this case. Once Y has had an opportunity to renew and solidify his relationship with her, it ought to be sufficient that time with the husband (and X) be gradually reintroduced leading up to week-end time and school holiday time. After four months, the introduction of supervised telephone time allows the relationship with the husband to continue with appropriate protection for the relationship with the wife. After six months, the introduction of supervised physical contact allows the relationship with the husband and X to resume in person, but still with some safeguards to prevent them consciously or subconsciously undermining the relationship between Y and the wife. After nine months from the date of the orders, the relationship between Y and the wife ought to be sufficient to allow fortnightly weekend time and half school holidays with the father, although I will limit the blocks of school holiday time over the 2026 Christmas break to a maximum of one week to ensure Y’s relationship with the wife is not undermined. By 2027 the relationships ought to be sufficient to withstand half of the school holidays.
It is apparent that the parties are unable to communicate effectively. The wife ought to have sole parental responsibility for Y, but consult with the husband. The parties can use a parenting app for communication and the husband ought to be able to receive school information.

Balance sheet issues in contention

A number of items remained in contention. Unfortunately, there was little cross-examination or evidence on these issues.

Item 8 – Motor Vehicle 1

Counsel for the husband submits that Motor Vehicle 1 has been sold (as the husband said in evidence) and that there is no reason to doubt that evidence.

Item 11 – Motor Vehicle 2 (purchased Nov 24)

The husband said in evidence that he had sold Motor Vehicle 2. His Financial Statement filed for the trial listed it as having a value of $20,000. He was not pressed in cross-examination as to who he sold it to, or about any transfer of registration, nor was any earlier financial statement put to him or tendered. I accept the figure that he set out in his financial statement as an admission and his evidence that he has sold the motor vehicle.

Item 13 – Water Vessel 2 and trailer (purchased 18.09.23)

Again, the husband’s evidence was that the water vessel and trailer have been sold for $90,000 and that he has spent the money. There is no evidence to challenge that, and no registration or other documents in evidence.

Item 14 – Water Vessel 3 and trailer

The husband’s evidence is that the water vessel and trailer have been sold and there is no evidence to the contrary, nor any documents or registration searches. The husband said that it was sold for between $24,000 and $25,000 and that the money had “gone back into the account” (Transcript 6 August 2025, p.295 line 10).

Item 17 – Mower, tools, push bikes, BBQ, fishing boat

Counsel for the husband submits that “this item appears to be an assertion by the Wife in relation to miscellaneous items and an estimated value that the Husband provided in a historic Financial Statement. There is no accounting of the items, valuation of the items nor adjustment for items which have otherwise been disposed of” (Summary of Closing Submissions on behalf of the Respondent Husband, as contained in Exhibit B, p.19). If the item is based upon an historic Financial Statement, it was not tendered into evidence. The husband said in cross‑examination that the mower had a value of $3,000 (Transcript 6 August 2025, p.296 line 14) however he said he has sold it. Counsel for the wife did not address this in her written submissions nor the notes to the last balance sheet (Exhibit 9A). The balance of the husband’s cross-examination on this topic was not of sufficient clarity to allow me to place any weight on it.

Item 18 – Cash

This appears to be based on a misunderstanding of the husband’s evidence about having sold an item for $20,000. However, there was evidence from the husband that he had $25,000 in cash at his home at the time he was cross-examined, which was the sale proceeds of Water Vessel 1 at trial which remain listed on the balance sheet without dispute at $25,000. The notes to the balanced sheet address a different issue – the proceeds of the husband’s personal injuries settlement.
Given the husband’s evidence in cross-examination that the water vessel was sold during the trial (Transcript 6 August 2025, p.293 lines 5–11), Item 9 should remain as cash proceeds are held by the husband from the sale.

Conclusion on Items 8, 11, 13, 14, 17 and 18

None of these items are property presently held by the husband. Thus, they are not assets. In light of the Full Court decision in Shinohara & Shinohara [2025] FedCFamC1A 126; (2025) FLC 94-266, they cannot be included in the list of property as ‘add-backs’.

Contributions assessment

The wife sought a contributions assessment ‘of up to’ 60 per cent in her favour in her Case Outline (at paragraph 33), although then sought:
Wife seeks orders that she retain the balance of the funds in the trust account and her superannuation, and the husband retain his funds and other assets.
In her final submissions (contained in Exhibit B) the wife sought “60% to 70%” based upon the findings she contended for that:
1.1 The wife made greater financial and non-financial contributions during the marriage and post separation [s79(4)(a-ca) Family Law Act 1975 (the Act)

1.2 The husband has ‘intentionally or recklessly’ caused ‘material wastage’ to the property or financial resources available to him or to both parties [s79(4)(d) of the Act]

1.3 The husband has been a perpetrator of family violence during and after the marriage which has had an adverse life altering impact on the wife and the children [s79(5)(a) of the Act]

1.4 The husband has undertaken a course of conduct to deliberating increase the liabilities and reduce the available pool [s79(5)(e) of the Act]

1.5 The husband has deliberately failed to comply with his duty of disclosure and has not provided the relevant financial disclosure to the wife or the court [s71B(1-2) of the Act].

1.6 Given the husband’s non-disclosure the court ought to make an adjustment of the property pool in the wife’s favour: (s71B(2)(a) of the Act; Weir and Weir [1992] FamCA 69; (1993) FLC 92-338: Black v Kellner [1992] FamCA 2; (1992) FLC 92-287.

1.7 The wife has continued to support the children financially and pays child support as assessed which is in the range of $700 per week ($2800 per month). [exhibit 14]

(as per the original)
The first of these claims is unsustainable as the husband’s personal injuries settlement was around $1,037,832 and the husband has been the primary carer of the children since separation. Much of the balance goes to factors pursuant to s 79(5).
The husband, in his Case Outline sought an assessment of 60 per cent to 40 per cent in his favour on contributions and an adjustment pursuant to s 79(5) of 10 per cent based upon his lower income earning capacity and care of the children into the future. In his written submissions he argued for a two-pool approach, treating the funds from the sale of the matrimonial home as one pool to which he argued a 55 per cent to 45 per cent assessment in his favour as a result of his post-separation care of the children. The other pool would be the compensation funds, which it is argued is the source of the funds for the various other items on the balance sheet. On this basis he argues that the interim payments to the wife represent approximately 23 per cent of the compensation pool, that this represents a sufficient split of these monies and that any waste allegations relate to the balance and therefore do not need to be determined. Counsel for the husband notes that (Summary of Closing Submissions on behalf of the Respondent Husband, as contained in Exhibit B, p.22):
This is, in a sense, a hypothetical exercise because on one view of things this logic is re-creating an asset pool which is no longer in existence in a way which may offend the logic in Shinohara. Having said that, however, of that hypothetical compensation pool the Husband would have argued that his contribution was greater than 77%. In all of the circumstances there is no need to make an adjustive Order in relation to Pool 2 and it would not be just and equitable to do so.
The husband argues that when considering the s 79(5) factors, a two pool approach should again be adopted, this time with respect to the proceeds from the sale of the matrimonial home (for which he seeks a 15 per cent adjustment based upon earning differences and care of the children) and the superannuation entitlements for which he seeks a 50/50 split of the wife’s superannuation entitlements from her Super Fund 1 account.
Whilst there is considerable logic in the approach suggested by the husband, it so fragments the considerations that it would be unlikely to result in the ‘holistic’ approach required by the Full Court authorities.
The relationship was for around 22 years. At commencement it appears that both parties had minimal assets.
During the relationship, both parties worked and applied their savings to the purchase of their first home in 2003 in Town G. Later, they sold the Town G property and purchased the Suburb H property. In 2012, the parties sold the Suburb H property and purchased the Town J property. The Town J property was sold less than a year later in 2013, and the parties purchased the former matrimonial home in Town E.
The wife was in paid employment, took six to eight months off on maternity leave to care for both children, and made significant household contributions.
The wife says that the husband worked from cohabitation until his accident in 2016 as a self‑employed tradesperson. Following the husband’s accident in 2016, which required him to be hospitalised followed by six months in a wheelchair and further time on crutches, the wife supported the family financially whilst also undertaking caring and household responsibilities.
Significantly, the husband received a large compensation payment for the consequences of his medical treatment. He says that it was in the order of $1,037,000, which does not appear to be disputed.
Since separation, the husband has been solely responsible for the care of the children, with the wife paying child support of around $660 per week. He has been on a low income but had the use of the home (on which he does not appear to have paid rates) until it was sold (noting that some of the rates debt appears to have existed at separation). The husband also received some modest financial support from his mother.
The value of the assets accumulated by the parties when together is largely comprised of the net sale proceeds of the home and the superannuation. Whilst the superannuation amount is agreed, the wife provided no evidence or submissions as to the amount of the net proceeds of the sale of the home or where they were applied. The husband, in his affidavit (at paragraphs 117 to 120) says that the net proceeds were $967,672. The balance sheet shows home contents of around $19,000 in total.
Thus, around $2,270,015 in assets and superannuation (the net proceeds of the home, the value of the furniture, the personal injuries settlement and $246,343 in superannuation) resulted from the contributions of the parties during the relationship. Whilst much of this is no longer available, s 79(4) requires regard to be had not only to existing assets but also contributions to assets no longer held by the parties when assessing contributions.
I am not persuaded that in the circumstances of this case the wife has established incidents of family violence that would justify taking them into account under s 79(4)(ca).
The contributions of the parties appear to be around 60/40 in favour of the husband, noting that the impact of many factors await consideration pursuant to s 79(5) to be taken into account.

Current and future circumstances of the parties – section 79(5) considerations

The wife raised a wastage argument, submitting:
The husband maintains that he spent over one million dollars in the last two years while he was receiving income protection payments of $1,286 per week, and $700 per week in child support from the wife.
The parties did not live a lavish lifestyle however, the husband seems to have drained exorbitant amount from the pool.
(wife’s case outline filed 1 August 2025, p.6, paragraphs 35–36, as per the original)
The wife also relied upon the husband’s less-than-full disclosure, making a submission for an adjustment in her favour:
The husband has failed to account for the dissipation of the funds and there is $330,000 unaccounted for even when if one accepts the husband's explanation for where the money went. The husband has refused to provide disclosure and consistently delayed providing information to the wife's legal representatives. For example, the husband only disclosed the exact amount he received from the compensation in February 2025 despite numerous requests.

(wife’s case outline filed 1 August 2025, p.6, paragraph 38)
I accept the wife’s claim that the husband has failed to make full and frank disclosure of how he dealt with all of the monies from his personal injuries settlement, although I accept that some of the monies would likely have been expended on day to day living expenses.
The wife portrays her future circumstances as appearing to be worse than the husband’s, arguing:
The wife has no other assets or financial resources and needs to find acquire housing and have some financial security.
The husband will receive income payments until the age of 67 and he has not provided any evidence that he does not have capacity to earn any further income.
(wife’s case outline filed 1 August 2025, p.7, paragraphs 46–47)
I am not persuaded that the husband’s income is ever likely to exceed that of the wife’s. He receives his insurance payments only while he cannot work. Those payments are lower than the income earned by the wife. There is no evidence upon which I could conclude that, if the husband recovers, he would be able to earn as much as the wife.
Whilst the origin of the tax debt is less than clear it appears to have largely arisen in the year that the parties separated and thus does not lead to an adjustment under s 79(5), save to the extent that interest and penalties have accrued during a period when the husband chose not to pay it despite having his personal injuries payment.
The husband is required to meet future medical costs for the injuries for which he has received compensation.
The wife will have the care of Y and the costs that this entails. The husband has the care of X, however he will soon be working (if he has not started a job already). Whilst X will not immediately be self-supporting (an apprentice does not receive significant income) the financial costs will be modest. Y will have to be supported for longer given the age difference.

Item 21 – Monies received from compensation payout

Item 21 is the amount that the wife submits is unaccounted for from the husband’s personal injuries settlement. It is difficult to make a finding as to a precise amount and difficult to determine where the monies all went (beyond legal fees). However, it is clear that the husband had control over these monies and there is little left, aside from the modest items in the balance sheet.

Item 22 and 23 and 26– Legal fees and interim property settlement

Whilst in the past legal fees funded from the property of the parties would have been added back to the balance sheet, the abolition of addbacks does not change the underlying principle: the parties should fund their own legal fees, rather than treating them as a joint debt. To the extent that they have used funds that would otherwise be in the balance sheet for legal fees, this must be taken into account. This also includes taking into account that debts for legal fees should be borne by the party liable for the fees, even though they may appear on the joint balance sheet.
Similarly, interim property settlements must be taken into account under s 79(5) now that they cannot be added back to the balance sheet.

Item 24 – Arrears in council rates

The final submissions of the wife provided little assistance on this issue. The wife simply assets that ‘[t]he husband also had sole occupancy of the former matrimonial home post separation until the property was sold and ceased making mortgage repayments, council rates and other outgoings notwithstanding he had ample financial resources to do so” (at paragraph 18 of her written submissions, as contained in Exhibit B, p.10).
Exhibit 32 shows a rates debt of $9,194.00 as at May 2024. The notice also shows quarterly instalments at $635.00. By the time the property was sold (settlement occurred in early December 2024) the rates debt had reached $13,533.77. The wife left the home in May 2023. Neither party has provided a basis for calculating the periods during which the rates debt accrued (a simple division by the quarterly amount would fail to take account of the nine per cent per annum daily interest charge imposed by the council). However, it is apparent that at least some part of the debt must have accrued whilst the parties were sharing the home.
I have already had regard to this issue (and the husband’s use of the home after separation) when considering contributions of the parties and therefore do not take the rates that accrued in that period into account a second time.

Item 25 – Z Company - car loan

The wife sets out the events at paragraphs 347 to 359 of her affidavit. She had a car loan in her name for the vehicle retained by the husband. The repayments by him were in default. This caused considerable angst as the financier was contacting the wife. Ultimately the debt was paid out from the sale proceeds of the home.
Each have had the use of funds (including partial property settlement amounts set out above) and paid significant legal fees.
I note that the parties cannot access superannuation for some years. The wife has a greater income than the husband and the husband has the possibility of future health costs. I am persuaded that in the circumstances of this case a superannuation splitting order is not appropriate, but that superannuation be taken up in the pool of assets generally, and the limited access to superannuation by the wife be taken into account pursuant to s 79(5).
More generally, it is clear that the husband has had control over his personal injuries settlement of over $1 million. There is no satisfactory accounting of how all of that money has been expended. The expenditure on boats and trailers appears to have been beyond reasonable hobbies given the circumstances of the parties. The remaining assets represent only a fraction of that amount. I accept that part of the money was expended on legal fees and part on reasonable living expenses for the husband and the children, however much was also spent in ways that have not been satisfactorily accounted for in the proceedings.

Conclusions on section 79(5)

There is no simple mathematical method of explaining the ultimate figure for the adjustment appropriate to take account of the various factors agitated by the parties.
I am persuaded that as a result of these various factors there should be an adjustment in the wife’s favour such that the remaining assets of the parties are split 60 per cent to 40 per cent in favour of the wife. This will result in the wife retaining 431,972$664,130.00 (being $431,972.00, together with $232,158.00 in superannuation) and the husband retaining the balance.

Just and equitable outcome – section 79(2) of the Act

The split of assets should be undertaken on the basis that the wife retains her superannuation and each of the assets in her possession, and remains responsible for her debts. This will result in a split of the assets such that the wife will retain (assuming the joint accounts are retained by the husband) the following:
